name: jsonwebtoken
what_it_does: Node.js implementation of JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for signing, verifying, and decoding JWTs per RFC 7519. Supports HMAC (HS256/HS384/HS512), RSA (RS256/RS512), RSA-PSS, and ECDSA algorithms. Used for stateless authentication tokens, API access tokens, and service-to-service auth in Node.js. The standard JWT library for Node.js, created and maintained by Auth0.
use_cases: Generate signed JWT access tokens for agent API authentication using HS256 or RS256 with configurable expiration and claims
Verify and decode incoming JWT tokens in agent API middleware to authenticate requests without database session lookups
Create service-to-service authentication tokens for agent microservices using asymmetric RS256 with public/private key pairs
Implement short-lived tokens for agent operations (one-time downloads, timed access) using jwt.sign() with exp claim
Build stateless agent session management where session data is encoded in verified JWT claims without server-side storage
not_for: Browser environments — use the jose library for isomorphic JWT handling with Web Crypto API support
OAuth2 full flows — use passport-jwt or oidc-client for complete OAuth2/OIDC authentication flows; jsonwebtoken is a primitive
Refresh token management — jsonwebtoken handles token signing/verification only; build refresh token rotation logic yourself
best_when: Building Node.js API authentication where JWT signing and verification is needed with configurable algorithms and claim validation.
avoid_when: You need browser/isomorphic JWT handling — use jose. For complete OAuth2 flows use Passport.js or auth libraries.

auth.notes: jsonwebtoken IS the auth primitive — it signs and verifies tokens. Secret keys and RSA keys are the authentication credentials.

pricing.notes: jsonwebtoken is open source and free, maintained by Auth0.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local cryptographic operations.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: JsonWebTokenError subtypes (TokenExpiredError, NotBeforeError, JsonWebTokenError) are specific and well-documented. Error messages include which claim failed validation. Expired token errors include exp and iat values for debugging.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Verification is deterministic — same token + secret produces same result. Signing with the same claims/secret/algorithm is idempotent for non-time-based claims.
known_agent_gotchas: jwt.verify() is synchronous by default and blocks the event loop for RSA key operations — use the callback form for async verification in high-throughput agents
TokenExpiredError means the token is expired and should NOT be trusted — never catch TokenExpiredError and proceed; issue a new token via refresh flow
Algorithm 'none' (unsigned tokens) is a known JWT attack vector — always verify the algorithm in the decoded header before calling verify(); jsonwebtoken rejects 'none' by default
Symmetric (HS256) and asymmetric (RS256) keys are completely different — passing an RSA public key to HS256 verify() or vice versa throws a cryptic error, not a helpful type mismatch
jwt.decode() does NOT verify the signature — it only decodes the payload for inspection; always use jwt.verify() for authentication; using decode() for auth is a critical security bug
Clock skew between services may cause immediately issued tokens to fail verification — use clockTolerance option (e.g., 10 seconds) for cross-service JWT validation

security_score_components.security_notes: Critical: always use jwt.verify(), not jwt.decode(), for authentication. Use RS256 asymmetric keys over HS256 shared secrets for multi-service architectures. Algorithm confusion attacks are prevented by always specifying allowed algorithms in verify().
